speaker: interviewer
text: Number four interview with Mr. Zoltan Wieder. You were on-- you were describing conditions in the cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- also there were my sister-in-law, and her mother and-- her mother, and her father, and her relatives, you know, a lot of people. In [NON-ENGLISH], in that station, in the SS, like I said, the Hungarians, first the gendarmes, came up. And they ask us to give up everything. If you have something, they will search us. And I went down in the station there-- I don't know how they-- I went down to get water in the station. And I got mixed up with people there, all kind of people. But-- and I was thinking now maybe I could escape at that time. But I had all my family there. So I wouldn't even think about this. Sorry. And then some people did gave up whatever they had. And I was thinking-- I was thinking, I'm not giving up anything that I had to. I said, they find it, they find it. I thought, maybe this will help me. I will have a little money someplace to buy some food or something. And then in 10 minutes, the SS came up, and says-- troops came up. And-- and they told us, we are going further, didn't say where. And then I remember my mother started to cry. And she said, I'm not going to see any more my son, my eldest son. And she felt already something is very, very bad. And she was crying. And she said, I'm not going to see him anymore. And I didn't understand it at that time. But she had a feeling already she will-- she will [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know in which direction you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't-- we didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We just knew the German took over. And the train starting rolling again. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] again, they were rolling-- they-- they-- once in a day or so, they stopped the trains. And they brought in a little water, no food at all, nothing. And they took us the bucket, the buckets. And we were rolling, I think, about another two days. And in three days, we arrived, I remember in the night, early morning. It was a screaming and yelling there. And the train stopped. And we saw the station Auschwitz in-- in-- in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it light enough? Or it was it dark?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was already early morning. I think it was-- it was-- it was dawn already. And then the doors opened. The [NON-ENGLISH] opened the doors, because they were locked. And we saw some people screaming in this customes, which we got also, the slave costume-- slave clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They with stripes, blue stripes. They had a hat, also with blue stripes. They were also Jews, but they were working there. They were already there in Auschwitz. They were working in the [GERMAN] in-- in-- at the crematoriums, and so on, and with the Germans-- with the Germans-- with the German SS, with dogs, and screaming, and yelling. So [GERMAN], go out, out, fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went down from the wagons, and put on the bags bags, whatever we had. And right away, they said, women, children, separate, and men separate. And at that time, we thought-- we didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought we came to a labor camp or something. And even we told-- I told-- we told, I remembering [INAUDIBLE] my father-- I was with my father, and my mother, and two sisters separate. We told them, we'll see you later on, we hoped so. We never saw them any more. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they took us to-- to a guard-- to a German officer, which I understand it was Mengele now. And he was just with his hand. And he sent us on the right side. We didn't know what this means. And I know my mother, my two sisters, they were sent to the left side. And they did took to the crematorium. And we went to the right side. And they took us into the camp. And I remember on the camp, it says outside, [GERMAN]. So, work makes you [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] free, free man, free in English too. And they took us there. And we were staying there on the-- for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Staying where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a field there, in the camp. And then they came some-- they took us to some-- to a place there, where there were some also Jews. They were already a long time there, Polish Jews, and other Jews. And they told us to take off everything, all clothing. So at that time, my hat with the money, and everything, whatever I had, everything. We took out everything. They took away everything from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were all naked there. And they said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then they started to shave us. Again, the head, and a stripe in the middle of the head, and they shaved us, and the body.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, you are going to take a shower. So they took us in a big shower room. And they you a shower, because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then they-- when we left the shower, outside-- it was cold at that time in Auschwitz. They gave us the slave costumes, that stripes, blue stripes, with the hat. And they shaved the women too, whoever-- the women who went to work. But we didn't see the women anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from there, they took us to-- they called it [GERMAN]. They were counting. And we were staying there on the-- on the place there, and on the field there, for hours and hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to stay ten in a line, ten, ten, ten in a line. We were staying there. I was with my father. He was in the front. I was after back of him. And that was from the morning until about 2:00 o'clock. [GERMAN], we were all waiting there. And a German came. He was counting. Then came back another German counting. And then they brought us some food, which was a soup. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They gave us-- yeah, they gave us a soup bowl, and a soup spoon. And we had to put it in our pants. And that was-- they said, that will be your dish. That's it. You don't get nothing else. And then they came. They brought some soup, which is-- I tried it. And it looks-- look like it was from grass, grass made. Even I didn't eat the last three days almost nothing, and I felt I'm going to throw up. And my father said, you must eat. Otherwise, you will not live. You must eat. We can't-- you must try, and try again, try. And I just couldn't. It was-- it was terrible. And, finally, I started eating it. It was-- And in the evening, they gave us a-- one slice of black bread, and a little jello-- jelly-- jello, that red jello. And they took us to the barracks. And we were sleeping. There were bunks there, 12 people on a bunk. And we didn't have pillows or nothing, just sleeping there on the-- and thank God, they left me the boots. I had the boots from home. I came in a boot. And my father came in a boot too. They left the boot. And-- and we put the boot under our head. And we slept there. We had a blanket that they called it horse blankets. And we went to sleep. And we were so crowded, we couldn't-- in the morning at 4:00 o'clock, they woke us up. And [GERMAN], they called at 4:00 o'clock in the morning. And we didn't-- we didn't-- my head was here, my foot was there, mixed up. We couldn't find-- you know, it was so crowded.

speaker: interviewer
text: What barrack were you in? Do you know what--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, I don't recall the barracks number, because they were all numbered there. But in Auschwitz, it was in the big Birkenau camp. That was Auschwitz, that Birkenau. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were only eight days in Auschwitz. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with the people that you came with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I were with some of them now. I was only with my father. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't see nobody else from my town, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are people from other countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, also from Hungary, but-- but from other places. I don't remember seeing any more the people from my town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barrack there was a couple. I don't know his name, because you are so little there, eight days, you know? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did he speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He spoke German. He spoke German. And I don't know if you was bad or good, this guy. You know, because some capos were bad. Some, they were very nice and everything. I really don't know, because I was very little there. And every day, we went out. We had the whole day outside in the rain. The whole day, it was raining, cold. And counting, they were counting the whole day you were there. And then the evening, they took you in-- in the barracks. And at 4:00 o'clock in the morning, they woke you up again out. And they-- they had to go to a washroom there about half naked, and in the cold water. Outside, there was some water, wash you a little bit. And you didn't have soap, nothing, you know, no anything, no toothpaste, anything like this, you know, nothing. And I remember I saw one day in another camp there were women there. And I saw some-- some from the girls from-- and women from my town. And I ask them where my sister and mother are. They said, they don't know. They took them to another place. And-- and this people there-- I forgot to tell you, they shaved us. And they were tell me, you see there, there were big chimneys, and burning there, and the smoke, big smoke there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, you see there? Your mother-- your mother, your sisters are dead already. I couldn't believe it. I couldn't-- I said, what is he talking? I just couldn't believe something like this could happen. And-- Was there a smell of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was some kind of smell. But we didn't know what this is. We Thought some chemical plant or something. We didn't know what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about in the bunks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bunks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't know what to think. We-- we-- we were so afraid. And we were thinking just, we don't know where they are taking us. And at that time, we didn't think we will escape. We-- we thought, our life is worth nothing now. And-- but we have to try. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were there eight days. And in the eighth day, they-- from-- they took us out to the field again in [INAUDIBLE]. And from there, they took us to-- to the trains, to the rail station. And they put us in-- it was raining, pouring, the whole day. And after we were outside for about eight hours, we were all wet, all wet. And-- because we had this-- this clothing was like pajamas, very thin, what we had. So we went-- they took us to the trains, the cattle wagons. And they put us in the cattle wagons. And they closed the doors. And we said at that time, if we are not going to get pneumonia now, never. And we were talking about this. And you didn't where we are going. And once we saw from the train Krakow. So-- so we said-- so we are going east someplace, to-- to Poland someplace, we didn't know. So-- and, finally, after two and a half or three days, we arrived to-- we saw the rail station Warsaw. So then they took us out from the trains. And with the SS behind us, with the SS. And actually, every three hours, you had the SS with their-- with the [NON-ENGLISH], with their-- and they took us to a camp. We didn't know what is. That was the Warsaw ghetto, we find out after that. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like when you came into it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looked like-- we saw the big buildings, walls, because when the-- in '43, when they destroyed the ghetto, they bombed the ghetto with incendiary bombs. And they did it-- the way the walls, the big walls, wouldn't be destroyed, because they wanted to have the bricks, and take them to Germany. So when we arrived to that ghetto, it was-- we had barracks there. There were two lagers-- a new lager, and an old, old camp lager, they called it, concentration camp. And a new one and old one, we were in the new one. All together, I understand there were 6,000 Jews at that time-- I mean slaves there in the-- in the ghetto. And we were put there in a barrack. I think my barrack, I think it was number one, if I remember good, number ten. And the capo was a German, which was a German criminal. He-- he was taken into that camp, because he killed, I understand, some of his family. And he had a black stripe, which means he was a criminal-- a criminal in that jail, in that camp actually. Oh, I forgot to tell you, in Auschwitz, we got a number. We were not tattooed. We got a number on-- my number was 4,321. And my father's was 4,320. And that number was put on my yellow star. Plus, on the head, we had a stripe. And plus, on the back of my jacket, there was also a big yellow stripe. You should be recognized, if you tried to escape or something, anyplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there in your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barrack in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, there it was better, because we-- we had, like, double-- triple banks, a bank with triple beds. And each of us had a bank, one bank, one bed. So I slept. And I remembered my father slept on the bottom. I slept in the middle, and on top somebody else. And we didn't have pillows there either. So we used our boots. And we had a-- we got a blanket, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we ate in the-- we ate in the barracks. We got-- actually, only in the evening and in the morning. And in the morning, we got-- we got a coffee, which was made from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not real coffee. It was a substitute coffee. And that was a black-- a black-- in the-- we didn't have cups. They put it in our bowl, the coffee-- and a cup of coffee. And we had-- the bread, we got a slice of bread in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we kept half to have it for the morning. And we put it away. And we put it under our hat, because happened some places where they stole the bread. How did people get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- people-- yeah, they got along. I mean, everybody was-- you know, there are no problems. But some place happened that they-- they stole-- I heard they stole bread here and there at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they help each other, people help each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- yeah, they tried. Some, they tried to help, whatever they could, you know? But everybody was-- was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to survive himself. And we got, in the morning, that coffee. And then in the morning when you wake up-- you woke up-- they woke us up at 4 o'clock all time. And they took us to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we went to the washroom, which were outside, to wash in the cold water. And then we got that coffee. And they too took us out for work. We went-- we went about I don't know how many kilometers to the part of the ghetto which was destroyed, with the guards. And we formed a chain every three yards, one guy. And we threw to each other the bricks. And first, we took out the bricks with ropes, the walls, brought down the walls. And then we started picking up the bricks, and throwing it, a long line and chain, like a chain, to a place. And we put it there in 500 at a time, 500 bricks in-- stacked it together. And then the Polish guys came in with wagons, with horse wagons. And they took us the bricks. And they took the bricks to Germany. I mean, the Germans, they sent it by train to Germany, to build there, to use it there. And with these Polish guys who came in, Polish workers, sometimes you could get a little bread from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know-- we-- we-- how we got some cigarettes from where and what. But for a cigarette or two, we got a little bread or something, you know? I don't know how we got it. But sometimes we got a little-- Did you have any money at all with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have nothing. I didn't have no money, because they took away everything. So I didn't have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were sick in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, thank God I wasn't, because you couldn't afford to be sick there, To because you would have been sick, they would have killed you right away. So I wasn't. And, you know, we went every morning out, and worked until in the evening at 7:00 o'clock or so. Then then brought us in. And you got a coffee in the evening again. And there-- lunch time, there you got your-- your soup, which was something like made from grass. And if you are lucky, you had a potato-- not peeled, just with everything in it. Then, you were very lucky. Then when you went out to the bathrooms-- the bathrooms were like outside, open. And you were talking to each other. Oh, I got-- oh I got today a potato my soup. That was a big deal already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody have special privileges in this group? Were there leaders in the group of any sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe the-- the capos had-- they did have some privileges. They had better food, more food. And they didn't have to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: The capo-- your capo was the criminal?

speaker: interviewee
text: My capo-- my capo in Warsaw was a German, and naturally didn't work, and had privileges. He was a very cruel man otherwise. But he had one thing. And he looked up the birthdays, whoever had a birthday. And he called them in. And if he was in good mood, he gave him a bread, a black bread, you know that military bread, and a piece of margarine. And if he was in bad mood, then he-- he beat them up, terribly. I remember July 25th was my birthday. And I was already about three weeks in Warsaw, four weeks, maybe about four, five weeks. And I was waiting for that. And I said, maybe I will get a bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did. He called me in. What's your name? How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very nice. And he gave me a bread, and a piece of margarine. So I went out, I gave my father, I gave some other people. So we were all-- we had a good day that time. That was a big thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody punished in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp, I remember there were people-- yeah, they were beaten up, some people there. And I understand they were-- they were hanged some people, too, which I didn't see. But-- and I remember, every day we heard shots. There were a wall there, a big wall. And behind the wall, as we went to work, we came back, we heard always shots-- shots, and shots. So they killed people. They shot people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there women in this camp at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were only men there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea of calendar? You knew when it was your birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I knew when it was my birthday. And-- and we were-- some way counting the days, some way. And sometimes we find out from the Poles. They came into the camp for the bricks. And we find out what days. We knew Sundays, Mondays this we knew, because we were counting all the time. But we found out from them what date is it actually. And there in Warsaw we were there about, I don't know, close to two months or so. And in the middle of August, the-- we didn't know that. But I understand the Russian troops, they came near Warsaw. They were near the Vistula River. The Vistula River is close to Warsaw. And they stopped there. And they came up to there. They stopped. The Germans then started evacuating the Warsaw ghetto-- actually, the camp. It was a concentration camp, not a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any Jewish holidays when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was aware. And I will get to this now. And I will tell you how it happened. In the concentration camp, one day they took us out to the-- to make us-- they didn't take us to work that morning. So we were-- had [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were counting in the morning. We were there in lines. And we thought at that time, they will kill us there. That's why they are holding us there. They don't take us out for work. So we said, that's it. And then the [GERMAN], which is the commander of the lager, came in. And he said, you are going to-- you are going to leave from here. And you are going to go to-- you are leaving from here. He didn't tell us where and what. And about 10:00 o'clock in the morning, the SS guards came in. And they were situated every three yards from each of us, from both sides, with their weapons. And they started taking us out from the lager, walking. I'm going to have to change our tape again.
